summary: Gruber pushes back on claims made in Brian Merchant's book about the iPhone's development, specifically a Tony Fadell anecdote portraying Phil Schiller as stubbornly insisting on a hardware keyboard. Gruber argues that even if true, the position was reasonable given the state of technology in 2005, before a viable software keyboard existed. He also challenges a quote from former Apple executive Brett Bilbrey dismissing Schiller's technical knowledge, calling it 'bullshit' based on his own off-the-record conversations with Schiller. Gruber notes that Fadell himself spent over a year working on click-wheel iPhone prototypes, undermining his credibility to mock others' misjudgments. The piece suggests these accounts reveal personal grudges more than historical truth.
key_points: Tony Fadell's claim that Schiller was a 'lone holdout' for a hardware keyboard comes from a single source who himself championed the click-wheel interface
Preferring a hardware keyboard in 2005 was reasonable — critics questioned the iPhone's software keyboard for years after launch
Gruber disputes the characterization of Schiller as technically unsophisticated, citing his own extensive on- and off-the-record conversations
Merchant's book may contain a factual error about Bilbrey's group name, conflating the Technology Advancement Group with the long-defunct Advanced Technology Group
Schiller's combative style has made enemies, and the book's sources appear to include several of them
money_quotes: Neither of those things was obvious in 2005.
For chrissakes there were critics who insisted that the iPhone's software keyboard wasn't good enough for years after the iPhone actually shipped.

This story about Phil Schiller pushing for a hardware keyboard comes from one source, and that one source is the guy who admittedly spent over a year working on iPhone prototypes with a click wheel interface.
Schiller's hard-charging, brusque style and his obvious political acumen have made him a lot of enemies over the years. It sounds like Fadell is one of them.
Especially off-the-record, Schiller can drill down on technical details to a surprising degree.
I don't know what Schiller did to piss off Bilbrey, but Bilbrey either has a huge chip on his shoulder or was severely misquoted by Merchant.
My guess is that Schiller was insisting that the iPhone needed to be a personal computer, not a mere gadget.
key_insight: Claims in tell-all tech histories deserve scrutiny of source motivations, especially when a single disgruntled former colleague's account is presented as definitive history.
